fuzzy_prd:
We need a small state-machine engine that can be attached to any plain object. The developer declares a set of named states, designates a starting state, and declares transitions as trigger-name + source-state + destination-state rules. Calling a trigger by name moves the object between states according to those rules. Each trigger should report whether it actually moved and what state is now active. A per-state predicate lets callers ask 'am I in state X right now?' Transitions can be guarded by boolean checks — some always pass, some always fail — and multiple transitions can share the same trigger name with the first eligible one winning. States can have enter and exit callbacks that run as side effects and mutate observable data on the object. Triggers can carry keyword arguments and the machine has a payload-delivery mode that controls whether callbacks receive those arguments directly or wrapped in an event object. The engine should also auto-generate a to_<state> convenience trigger for every state by default, with an option to disable that. Handle it the way similar ordered-transition helpers are handled internally — a bulk generator that wires up a linear chain of transitions under one trigger name.

One extra bit of color from the team’s questions: when a trigger actually goes through, it should emit exactly 'trigger=<name> result=true state=<new_state>'. If the trigger exists but doesn’t get to move because the guards didn’t pass, it should emit exactly 'trigger=<name> result=false state=<unchanged_state>'. An invalid transition emits: 'error=invalid_transition trigger=<name> state=<current_state>'. A missing trigger emits: 'error=no_trigger trigger=<name>'.

Also on the callback side, the examples are meant to be literal. greet() records the text 'Hello World!' in model.message. farewell() records 'So long, suckers!' in model.message. store_kwarg_message(message=...) records the 'message' kwarg value directly. store_event_message(event_data) reads message from event_data.kwargs['message'] when send_event=True.

And one more clarification around the generated helpers: when auto_transitions=False, the to_<state> triggers simply do not exist. So if somebody tries to fire one anyway, the result should be 'error=no_trigger trigger=to_C' (or whichever state name was used). No move happens and no exception leaks to stdout.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: class Machine(model, states, initial=None, transitions=None, send_event=False, auto_transitions=True, ordered_transitions=False, **kwargs)

trigger_keywords: what are the named condition checks
what does cond_pass return vs cond_fail
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: cond_pass always returns True (the transition fires). cond_fail always returns False (the transition is blocked). Guards in 'conditions' require all to be True. Guards in 'unless' require all to be False.

trigger_keywords: what is the is_state predicate output format
how is a state query formatted
what line does the state op produce
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'state' op produces: 'state=<current_state>'. The 'is_state' op produces: 'is_state=<state_name> value=<true|false>'. The 'get_message' op produces: 'message=<recorded_message>'.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: Handle it the way similar ordered-transition helpers are handled internally — a bulk generator that wires up a linear chain
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Machine.add_ordered_transitions() in transitions/core.py generates a linear chain of transitions through all registered states under a single trigger (default: 'next_state'), with loop-back to the first state. It accepts custom state ordering, trigger name, and loop_includes_initial flag.

oracle_data.fallback_response: Please refer to the start.md specification. The machine is built from a 'machine' spec and a 'commands' list. Each command has an 'op' field selecting the operation.

feature1_basic_transitions.json.description: A machine is created over a set of named states with one designated as the starting state. Explicitly declared transitions wire a trigger name to a (source -> destination) edge. Firing a trigger while in the matching source state moves the machine to the destination state; the active state can be queried directly, and per-state predicates report whether a given state is the current one. Each fired trigger reports whether it caused a move and the resulting active state.

cases.expected_output:
trigger=advance result=true state=B
state=B
is_state=A value=false
is_state=B value=true
trigger=advance result=true state=C
state=C

feature2_conditional_transitions.json.description: A transition can be guarded by conditions. A `conditions` guard requires every named boolean check to return true for the transition to fire; an `unless` guard requires every named check to return false. When a guard blocks a transition, firing the trigger reports no move and the active state is unchanged. When several transitions share the same trigger and source, they are evaluated in declaration order and the first whose guards all pass is taken. `cond_pass` is a check that always returns true and `cond_fail` always returns false.

cases.expected_output:
trigger=advance result=true state=B
state=B
trigger=advance result=true state=C
state=C
trigger=advance result=false state=C
state=C

feature3_lifecycle_callbacks.json.description: States can carry lifecycle callbacks that fire as a side effect of transitions: an enter callback runs when a state is entered, an exit callback runs when a state is left. Callbacks are referenced by name and mutate observable model data. `greet` records the text 'Hello World!' and `farewell` records the text 'So long, suckers!'. Enter/exit callbacks can be registered after the machine is built, or declared inline on a state at construction time. The recorded message can be read back to confirm a callback fired.

cases.expected_output:
trigger=advance result=true state=B
message=Hello World!
trigger=reverse result=true state=A
message=So long, suckers!

feature4_event_payload.json.description: Triggers may carry a payload of positional/keyword arguments. The machine has a payload-delivery mode controlling how that payload reaches transition callbacks. When delivery is disabled, the trigger's keyword arguments are forwarded directly to the callback signature; `store_kwarg_message` records the value supplied under the keyword `message`. When delivery is enabled, callbacks instead receive a single event object that wraps the arguments; `store_event_message` reads the same `message` value out of that event object's keyword payload. Either way the recorded message reflects the value passed at trigger time.

kwargs.message: Hallo. My name is Inigo Montoya.

cases.expected_output:
trigger=advance result=true state=B
message=Hallo. My name is Inigo Montoya.

kwargs.message: You killed my father. Prepare to die.

cases.expected_output:
trigger=advance result=true state=B
message=You killed my father. Prepare to die.

feature5_auto_transitions.json.description: When automatic transitions are enabled, the machine provides a convenience trigger named `to_<state>` for every registered state that moves to that state from any source. When automatic transitions are disabled, no such convenience trigger exists and attempting to fire one is reported as a missing-trigger error. The active state is reported after each successful automatic transition.

cases.expected_output:
trigger=to_B result=true state=B
state=B
trigger=to_C result=true state=C
state=C
trigger=to_A result=true state=A
state=A

feature6_ordered_transitions.json.description: A helper wires up a chain of transitions that step linearly through a sequence of states under a single advancing trigger (default name `next_state`). By default it loops the last state back to the first. When no starting state is supplied at construction, an implicit placeholder starting state is created and included in the chain. The loop-back target can be configured to skip that placeholder. The chain can also be built from an explicit custom ordering with a custom trigger name, or requested directly at construction time. Each advance reports the resulting active state.

cases.expected_output:
state=initial
trigger=next_state result=true state=beginning
state=beginning
trigger=next_state result=true state=middle
trigger=next_state result=true state=end
state=end
trigger=next_state result=true state=initial
state=initial

trigger=advance result=true state=end
state=end
trigger=advance result=true state=beginning
state=beginning
